# Shipping a Replication Package

Chapter 8 closed a promise from Chapter 1: a reproducible project is
code, plus a recipe for its data, plus a locked environment. This
chapter turns that working project into something you hand to a
stranger. A **replication package** is the self-contained bundle a
referee, a journal data editor, or a researcher three years from now
downloads and runs to regenerate every number and figure in your paper.
It is the same code, data recipe, and environment from Chapter 8, but
organized, documented, licensed, frozen at a version, and deposited
somewhere permanent.

Almost nothing here is a new Git command. Tagging a version is Chapter 7;
pushing that tag to GitHub is Chapter 9; turning it into a published
release is Chapter 11; the data and environment rules are Chapter 8.
What is new is the discipline of assembly: deciding what goes in, in
what structure, with what documentation, so that "it reproduces on my
machine" becomes "it reproduces on the data editor's machine."

::: {.callout-important .reproducibility title="REPRODUCIBILITY"}
The test a package must pass is blunt: a competent stranger, given only
your package and no access to you, can reproduce your results. Not "the
code is here somewhere" but "run these steps, in this environment, and
Table 3 comes back." Every choice below serves that one test. If a step
needs a tacit thing only you know, the package is not done.
:::

## What goes in: a defensible structure {.tier-beginner}

The package should be legible before it is run. Two widely used
conventions in the social sciences point the same way. The **TIER
Protocol** (version 4.0) organizes a project into `Data/`, `Scripts/`,
and `Output/`, driven by a single master script that runs the whole
pipeline end to end (verified 2026-06-23). Gentzkow and Shapiro's *Code
and Data for the Social Sciences* gives the principles behind that
shape: separate inputs from outputs, give each step its own place, and
automate the build so it runs top to bottom with no manual fiddling.

The running example already has this shape. Looking at only the tracked
files (not the generated data, which is rebuilt) shows the whole
package:

```bash
$ git ls-files
.gitignore
README.md
output/tables/did_main.txt
pyproject.toml
report.qmd
scripts/00_make_data.py
scripts/01_clean.py
scripts/02_analysis.py
uv.lock
```

Read that as a package. The `scripts/` run in numbered order
(`00_make_data` then `01_clean` then `02_analysis`): for this small
example, the numbered scripts play the role TIER assigns to a master
script, a reader runs them top to bottom. `pyproject.toml`
plus `uv.lock` are the locked environment (Chapter 8). `report.qmd` is
the write-up. The raw and cleaned data are deliberately absent, kept out
by `.gitignore`, because they rebuild from `00_make_data.py` under a
fixed seed. The one committed output, `did_main.txt`, is the headline
table, so a reader can diff what they regenerate against what you
reported.

::: {.callout-important .reproducibility title="REPRODUCIBILITY"}
Numbered scripts that run in sequence are a poor researcher's master
script, and they are enough. The reader needs exactly one thing: an
unambiguous order to run things in. Whether you provide it as `00_`,
`01_`, `02_` filenames, a `Makefile`, or a `run_all.sh`, the rule is the
same: no human judgement about what to run next should be required.
:::

## A README a stranger can actually run {.tier-beginner}

The README is the package's instruction manual, and it is where most
replication attempts fail. The social-science data editors maintain a
cross-journal **template README** (current version v1.1) that encodes
what belongs there (verified 2026-06-23). Its use is strongly
encouraged rather than strictly required, but its checklist is the
useful part. A package README should state where the data come from,
what data are provided and what are not, the software used *with the
exact version numbers as run*, any extra packages and their versions,
the hardware used, the wall-clock run time, and step-by-step
instructions that separate data preparation from analysis, plus a map
from each output file to the table or figure it produces.

The example's own README already covers the runnable core: it ships no
data, names the seeded rebuild, pins the environment, and states the
result to expect.

```markdown
## Reproduce the running example

The example ships no data; it rebuilds from a seeded
generator under a locked uv environment.

    uv run python scripts/00_make_data.py
    uv run python scripts/01_clean.py
    uv run python scripts/02_analysis.py

The difference-in-differences estimate reproduces
exactly: treat_post = -0.0189 (SE 0.0038), against a
true simulated effect of -0.018.
```

The single most valuable line in any replication README is the expected
result. Stating `treat_post = -0.0189` up front means a replicator knows
instantly whether their run succeeded, instead of staring at output
wondering if a small difference is a bug or a rounding artifact.

::: {.callout-warning .pitfall title="PITFALL"}
The most common README failure is the unstated dependency: "just run the
script" when the script needs a package, a data file, or an environment
variable you never mention because it has always been present on your
machine. The fix is mechanical: the locked environment (Chapter 8) is
what makes the software list complete and honest, and the cleanest test
of the README is to follow it yourself on a fresh clone, touching
nothing you would not ship. If your own instructions do not work from a
clean checkout, they will not work for a referee.
:::

## License and citation: the two small files {.tier-beginner}

Two short files decide whether others may legally use your package and
how they credit it. Without a license, the default is that no one may
reuse your code at all, so the omission is not neutral. Research packages
commonly carry two licenses because they contain two kinds of thing:
permissive terms for the code (MIT, BSD, Apache-2.0) and a content
license for the prose and figures (often Creative Commons). This book's
own repository does exactly that, MIT for code and CC BY 4.0 for the
writing, stated plainly in the README:

```markdown
- Code (scripts, tools/, the example) is released under
  the MIT License. See LICENSE.
- Book prose and figures are released under Creative
  Commons Attribution 4.0 (CC BY 4.0).
```

Citation is the companion. A `CITATION.cff` file in the repository root,
written in the Citation File Format, tells people how to cite the work,
and GitHub reads it automatically: with `cff-version: 1.2.0` and the
core fields present, GitHub shows a "Cite this repository" link in the
sidebar and renders the metadata as APA and BibTeX (verified
2026-06-23). The book's own file is the worked example:

```yaml
cff-version: 1.2.0
message: "If you use this book or its example, please cite it as below."
title: "Git and GitHub for Social Science Researchers"
type: book
authors:
  - family-names: "Luo"
    given-names: "Emma"
url: "https://github.com/tensor-polinomics/git-github-guide"
license: CC-BY-4.0
version: "0.1.0"
date-released: "2026-06-21"
```

That file is plain text under version control like everything else, so
the citation metadata travels with the code and updates in the same
commits.

## Freezing a version: the tagged release {.tier-advanced}

A paper cites a *specific* state of the code, not whatever `main`
happens to be the day a reader clones it. The mechanism is a tag
(Chapter 7) promoted to a release (Chapter 11). The local half is an
ordinary annotated tag, and it is fully reversible until you push it:

```bash
$ git tag -a v1.0.0 \
    -m "Replication package for the minimum-wage DiD note"
$ git tag -n
baseline-4commits Four-commit baseline for the Ch.5/6/16 demos (pre-environment)
v1.0.0          Replication package for the minimum-wage DiD note
```

The tag is a permanent, human-named pointer at one commit. Inspecting it
confirms exactly what state it freezes:

```bash
$ git show v1.0.0 --stat
tag v1.0.0
Tagger: A Researcher <researcher@example.edu>
Date:   Tue Jun 23 19:03:27 2026 +0000

Replication package for the minimum-wage DiD note

commit c1c408cdd55e49a24c9c1303c8d67468eba4d230
Author: Pat Researcher <pat@example.edu>
Date:   Mon Jun 22 02:10:36 2026 +0000

    Pin the software environment with a uv lockfile
```

`v1.0.0` points at the environment-lock commit, so the frozen version is
the complete code-plus-locked-environment state. Use semantic-style tags
(`v1.0.0`) so a later correction can be `v1.0.1` without ambiguity about
which version a result came from.

::: {.callout-tip .recovery title="RECOVERY"}
A local tag is cheap to fix before it is shared. Tagged the wrong commit?
`git tag -d v1.0.0` deletes it and you re-tag. Moved it? Same delete and
recreate. This easy reversibility ends the moment the tag is pushed and
turned into a public release that others may have cited, which is the
whole reason the publish step below is quarantined. Get the local tag
right while it is still free to change.
:::

A GitHub **release** attaches that tag to a downloadable, citable
artifact on the server (Chapter 11). Creating one changes public state
and is the kind of action this book runs deliberately, never in a
copy-paste block:

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
**Quarantined: publishing the release.** Run these one at a time, after
the local tag is exactly right. Each changes public state.

```text
# push the tag to GitHub
git push origin v1.0.0

# create a release from it (gh CLI, or the web UI)
gh release create v1.0.0 \
  --title "v1.0.0 replication package" \
  --notes "Code + locked environment for the DiD note."
```

`gh release create` publishes a tag the world can cite; treat it like
any other hard-to-undo action and run it knowingly.
:::

## A permanent, citable DOI: Zenodo {.tier-advanced}

A GitHub release can still be edited or deleted, and a repository can
move or vanish. For the *version of record* behind a paper, you want a
deposit that is permanent and carries a DOI. **Zenodo** integrates with
GitHub for exactly this: you connect Zenodo to GitHub and switch a
repository "on," and thereafter each new GitHub release triggers Zenodo
to archive the repository at that tag and mint a DOI (verified
2026-06-23). Zenodo issues two DOI kinds: a **version DOI** for each
specific release, and a **concept DOI** that always resolves to the
latest version. For reproducibility, cite the **version DOI**, because a
result was produced by one specific version; reserve the concept DOI for
referring to the software in general, when you do not mean a particular
release. The default
per-record limit is 100 files and 50 GB (raisable to 200 GB via the EU
Open Research Repository), which is ample for a code package whose data
rebuild from a script.

Because a published Zenodo record is permanent and citable, this is the
most consequential step in the chapter, and the book does not perform it.

::: {.callout-warning .pitfall title="PITFALL"}
A Zenodo DOI is forever. A published record cannot be deleted, only
superseded by a new version, so the first DOI you mint must not point at
a draft you are still revising. Mint the DOI when the package is actually
final: the paper accepted, the results frozen, the README tested on a
clean clone. Minting early to "get a DOI" leaves a permanent citable
pointer to work you later changed, which is precisely the confusion DOIs
exist to prevent.
:::

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
**Quarantined: minting the DOI.** Documented, not run in this book. Do
this only when the package is final.

```text
# one-time: at zenodo.org, log in with GitHub and flip
# the repository switch to "on".

# then, for the version you want archived, publish a
# GitHub release (the quarantined step above). Zenodo
# ingests it automatically and assigns the DOI.

# finally, add the DOI badge Zenodo provides to your
# README so readers find the archived record, not a
# moving branch.
```

No DOI was minted for this repository. A real release or DOI happens
only on a deliberate decision that the package is final.
:::

## Meeting a journal's data editor {.tier-advanced}

For many economics and finance journals the package is not optional and
not self-graded. The **American Economic Association** requires authors
of accepted empirical, simulation, or experimental work to deposit data,
code, and documentation, and the AEA Data Editor verifies that the
package actually reproduces the paper's results *as a condition of
publication*: a package that fails the check holds up the article. The
strongly encouraged, default home is the AEA Data and Code Repository at
**openICPSR**; other trusted repositories are accepted if they meet the
same display and reproducibility criteria (verified 2026-06-23). The
Econometric Society and other social-science journals run closely
related policies, which is why the cross-journal template README exists.

Two requirements deserve emphasis because they connect back to earlier
chapters. First, a **Data Availability Statement** is mandatory, and when
data cannot be shared (confidential or identifying microdata, or data
under a use agreement that bars redistribution), the statement must say
so and explain why. That is the Chapter 6 rule, never commit restricted
data, surfacing as a publication requirement: you ship the code that
*would* process the data plus a precise description of how a reader
obtains it, not the data itself. Second, the editor checks that the
package *actually runs*, which is the locked environment and seeded
rebuild from Chapter 8 doing their job under formal scrutiny.

::: {.callout-important .reproducibility title="REPRODUCIBILITY"}
A data availability statement is not a loophole for "the data are
secret, trust me." It is a precise, checkable claim: which dataset, from
which provider, under what access terms, at what cost or application
process, so an authorized reader can obtain the same data and run your
unchanged code. Restricted data is a reason to document access carefully,
not a reason to skip reproducibility.
:::

## What you can now do

You can take a working, reproducible project and ship it: organize it
into a legible structure (TIER or Gentzkow-Shapiro), write a README that
runs from a clean clone, license the code and the prose, add a
`CITATION.cff` so people cite it correctly, freeze the exact state as a
tagged release, and (when the work is truly final) archive it to a
permanent DOI, all the way through a journal data editor's checks. None
of it required a new Git concept, only the discipline to assemble what
Chapters 6, 7, 8, 9, and 11 already gave you. Chapter 16 turns to a newer
risk to all of this: what changes when an AI agent is driving Git.

## Try it yourself {.unnumbered}

Working in your clone of the running example:

1. Run `git ls-files` and write a one-line gloss for each tracked path,
   explaining what role it plays in the package (script, environment
   lock, expected output, documentation). Note which files are
   deliberately *absent* and why.
2. Follow the README's reproduce steps from scratch on a fresh clone,
   changing nothing you would not ship. Confirm `treat_post = -0.0189`
   comes back. If anything failed, fix the README, not your memory.
3. Tag the current state `v1.0.0` with an annotated tag, then inspect it
   with `git show v1.0.0 --stat`. Now delete it with `git tag -d v1.0.0`
   and explain in one sentence why deleting a local tag is safe but
   deleting a published release or DOI is not.
4. Draft a three-line Data Availability Statement for a hypothetical
   version of this project that used confidential county employment
   microdata instead of simulated data: name the (made-up) provider, the
   access terms, and how a replicator would request it.
